coredata.dc:title: AI-powered ultrasonic thermometry for HIFU therapy in deep organ
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Luan, Shunyao
Ji, Yongshuo
Liu, Yumei
Zhu, Linling
Zhao, Hong
Zhou, Haoyu
Li, Ke
Zhu, Weizhen
Zhu, Benpeng
coredata.dc:description: High-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) is considered as an important non-invasive way for tumor ablation in deep organs. However, accurate real-time monitoring of the temperature field within HIFU focal area remains a challenge. Although ultrasound technology, compared with other approaches, is a good choice for noninvasive and real-time monitoring on the temperature distribution, traditional ultrasonic thermometry mainly relies on the backscattered signal, which is difficult for high temperature (>50 °C) measurement. Given that artificial intelligence (AI) shows significant potential for biomedical applications, we propose an AI-powered ultrasonic thermometry using an end-to-end deep neural network termed Breath-guided Multimodal Teacher-Student (BMTS), which possesses the capability to elucidate the interaction between HIFU and complex heterogeneous biological media. It has been demonstrated experimentally that two-dimension temperature distribution within HIFU focal area in deep organ can be accurately reconstructed with an average error and a frame speed of 0.8 °C and 0.37 s, respectively. Most importantly, the maximum measurable temperature for ultrasonic technology has been successfully expanded to a record value of 67 °C. This breakthrough indicates that the development of AI-powered ultrasonic thermometry is beneficial for precise HIFU therapy planning in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Deep learning
High Intensity focused ultrasound
Noninvasive acoustic energy distribution monitoring
Respiratory compensation
Ultrasonic cavitation

U22A20259, 12304512), the Shenzhen Basic Science Research (No. JCYJ20220530160602006, JCYJ20230807143615023), the Interdisciplinary Research Program of Hust 2023JCYJ043, the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2022M721258), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. YCJJ20241406). We also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of Huazhong University of Science & Technology.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The AI-powered ultrasonics thermometry for assessing temperature distribution within the HIFU focal area in deep organs (a) A total of five New Zealand rabbits were selected for the animal experiments, and the HIFU imaging-treatment system was employed to acquire data. Iterative training and Knowledge Distillation were used for model training and inference. (b) The training process of temperature monitoring strategy. (c) SKL monition on temperature distribution within HIFU focal area in deep organ.

(a) Schematic diagram of the experimental system. It comprises a temperature detection system and a HIFU treatment system. The HIFU treatment system consists of a therapeutic imaging applicator, HIFU driving console and unit, ultrasound machine, and UI console. (b) Integrated therapeutic-imaging applicator. (c) Intubation of the rabbit, with the ventilator replacing the rabbit's spontaneous breathing. (d) Schematic position of the thermocouple insertion.

The framework of BMTS, which consists of three modules: RSP, MTKI, and SKL. The RSP module utilizes NCC coefficients, frequency domain filtering, and phase analysis algorithms to establish a mapping relationship between RF data and six respiratory stages. Next, the HIFU acoustic parameters were used as priors for the MTKI module, which employs the IN and SFF strategy, combined with a feature fusion algorithm, to capture the spatial feature maps of RF data. Finally, the SKL module decouples the association between
x
and the temperature in the HIFU focal area.

(a) The NCC of the first frame of RF data
x
from the inspiratory stage with all frames
M
. (b) the spectra of NCC, where P1 and P3 correspond to the respiratory and cardiac frequencies, respectively. (c) shows the smoothed NCC curve obtained using a low-pass filter to remove interferences other than respiratory motion. The red ROI area in (c) is magnified in (d), where the blue and red points represent the boundaries of the inhalation and exhalation cycles, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The results of temperature distribution within HIFU focal area in deep organ. The results of BMTS,
BMT
S
RSP
, and
BMT
S
MTKI
are shown in the first, second, and third rows, respectively.

The interpolated intensity profile of the green dashed line in Fig. 6. (a) and (b), (c) and (d), and (e) and (f) represent the lateral and axial interpolated intensity profile of the results for BMTS,
BMT
S
RSP
, and
BMT
S
MTKI
, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison between predicted temperature and thermocouple-measured temperature.
Performance of BMTS under different acoustic parameters.

head.ce:title: AI-powered ultrasonic thermometry for HIFU therapy in deep organ

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Integrated Circuits, Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Integrated Circuits, Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: HIFU Center of Oncology Department, Huadong Hospital Affiliated to Fudan University, Shanghai, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huadong Hospital Affiliated to Fudan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: HIFU Center of Oncology Department, Huadong Hospital Affiliated to Fudan University, Shanghai, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhonghui Medical Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhonghui Medical Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) is considered as an important non-invasive way for tumor ablation in deep organs. However, accurate real-time monitoring of the temperature field within HIFU focal area remains a challenge. Although ultrasound technology, compared with other approaches, is a good choice for noninvasive and real-time monitoring on the temperature distribution, traditional ultrasonic thermometry mainly relies on the backscattered signal, which is difficult for high temperature (>50 °C) measurement. Given that artificial intelligence (AI) shows significant potential for biomedical applications, we propose an AI-powered ultrasonic thermometry using an end-to-end deep neural network termed Breath-guided Multimodal Teacher-Student (BMTS), which possesses the capability to elucidate the interaction between HIFU and complex heterogeneous biological media. It has been demonstrated experimentally that two-dimension temperature distribution within HIFU focal area in deep organ can be accurately reconstructed with an average error and a frame speed of 0.8 °C and 0.37 s, respectively. Most importantly, the maximum measurable temperature for ultrasonic technology has been successfully expanded to a record value of 67 °C. This breakthrough indicates that the development of AI-powered ultrasonic thermometry is beneficial for precise HIFU therapy planning in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Power ultrasound transmits energy via high-intensity sound waves and holds substantial potential for clinical applications. A classic example is High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound (HIFU), which is widely used for non-invasive tumor ablation in deep organs [1]. During HIFU therapy, biological tissues absorb acoustic energy and convert it into heat. Then the temperature within the HIFU focal area increases rapidly, resulting in coagulative necrosis of the tumor tissue [2]. Therefore, controlling tissue temperature is critical for enhancing therapeutic efficacy. Despite significant clinical advancements in HIFU technology, non-invasive and accurate real-time monitoring of the temperature field within the HIFU focal area remains a fundamental scientific challenge [3].

Unlike other temperature measurement techniques such as thermocouples and magnetic resonance imaging, ultrasound combines real-time and non-invasive features, as well as ease of integration with HIFU treatment systems [3–5]. Consequently, these benefits make ultrasound an ideal choice for monitoring the temperature field within the lesion area [6]. Traditional ultrasonic monition on temperature field in the focal area of HIFU mainly relies on the backscattered signal, since tissue characteristicparameters such as ultrasound attenuation, sound speed, density, and thermal expansion are typically represented as linear functions of temperature within the range of 37 °C to 50 °C [5,7,8]. However, the non-linear relationship will appear when the temperature exceeds 50 °C, thus limiting its applications. Actually, during HIFU therapy, it is crucial to control the temperature in the focal area to surpass 60 °C to ensure effective coagulative necrosis [9]. Consequently, to solve the contradiction between the existingtechnology and clinical needs, exploring a new ultrasonic thermometry and expanding the temperature measurement range is indispensable.

Recently, artificial intelligence (AI) [10] has shown significant potential in solving clinical medical problems from image recognition [11], and segmentation [12] to treatment planning [13]. Hence, it is worth investigating whether AI-based ultrasound technology enables addressing the challenge of monitoring high-temperatures (T > 50 °C) within the focal area of HIFU in deep organs. Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, such research has been rarely carried out.

Here, we propose an AI-powered ultrasonic thermometry using an end-to-end deep neural network termed Breath-guided Multimodal Teacher-Student (BMTS). The BMTS model is proved to possess the capability to elucidate the interaction between HIFU and complex heterogeneous biological media. After diminishing physiological noise, two-dimension temperature distribution within HIFU focal area in rabbet’s liver can be accurately reconstructed with an average error and a frame speed of 0.8 °C and 0.37 s, respectively. Most importantly, the temperature measurement range of ultrasonic technology has been successfully expanded from 37 °C-50 °C to 37 °C-67 °C. This breakthrough suggests that AI-powered ultrasonic thermometry is a promising solution to monitor temperature change during HIFU ablation for deep organs.

The proposed ultrasonic thermometry is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Ultrasonic echo signals are obtained using an ultrasound imaging system and the reference temperature data are measured by thermocouples. The BMTS model was iteratively trained using a tenfold cross-validation method. The distilled Student Knowledge License (SKL) was then used to reconstruct the temperature field for clinical validation. Fig. 1(b) shows the training process of the MTKI, where ultrasound data, respiratory phase, and HIFU acoustic parameters serve as inputs to obtain the corresponding learned features. These learned features guide the direction of SKL weight updates, as shown in Fig. 1(c). Finally, the SKL reconstructs the temperature field using a sliding window algorithm.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the experimental setup, where a multi-thermocouple-based reference temperature detection system (TCP-16XL, Spectra Instruments Co., Ltd., China) and an ultrasound imaging system (OPEN480, Zhonghui Medical Technology Co., Ltd., China) were integrated and synchronized with the HIFU system. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the 1.5 MHz HIFU transducer with a curvature radius of 78 mm has a hole in the center, where a 6 MHz 128-element array was positioned for ultrasound data acquisition. The HIFU treatment parameters are acoustic power of 90 W, pulse repetition period of 800 ms, duty cycle of 50 %, and 300 pulses. The focal area of the HIFU beam is measured to be like an ellipsoid [14], with an axial length of 8 mm and a diameter of 2 mm.

The animal experiments were conducted following the protocol approved by the Animal Welfare and Ethics Group of the Department of Laboratory Animal Science, Fudan University (202310034S). Five New Zealand rabbits (weight: 4.5 ± 1 kg) were selected for the animal experiments, and were anesthetized via intramuscular injection of 1.3 ml of xylazine. After establishing venous access in the rabbit's marginal ear vein, 2–3 ml of vecuronium bromide was administered intravenously to achieve muscle relaxation. Following anesthesia induction, the rabbits were intubated and connected to a ventilator to replace spontaneous breathing (Fig. 2(c)). Anesthesia was maintained with isoflurane at a rate of 0.6 ml/min. The inhalation-to-expiration time ratio (I/E ratio) was set at approximately 0.5, and the respiratory rate was maintained at 30 breaths per minute. Both parameters were adjusted to maintain oxygen saturation above 95 %. The rabbits were placed in a supine position on a board with their forelimbs crossed and secured. Due to the desired tissue thickness and without major blood vessels, the middle lobe of the liver was chosen for HIFU experiment. The chest and upper abdomen were shaved using a trimmer and depilatory cream to expose the skin.

After being disinfected with 75 % alcohol, the shaved area was marked with three points with an interval of 2 cm for thermocouple insertion. Then, the commercial 15G puncture needle with a cannula was employed for operating and positioning temperature measuring point in the liver. When the needle was withdrawn, the thermocouple was inserted through the cannula, labeled as TC1, TC2, and TC3, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2(d). Guided by ultrasound imaging, the HIFU transducer can be adjusted to ensure that three thermocouples are perpendicular to its acoustic beam and TC1 is located near the focal point. During the HIFU ablation, the reference temperature was recorded by thermocouple (sampling rates: 10 Hz), meanwhile, the 128-element array was utilized to receive ultrasound echo signal (Radio Frequency (RF) data) with a sampling rate of 24 MHz. The ultrasound images were captured at a fixed frame rate of 9 FPS, and covered a depth range of 0–12 cm, with a frame size of 128 (lateral) × 2078 (axial) pixels. In the end, the rabbits were euthanized by air embolism (20–40 ml of air) via the marginal ear vein.

The proposed BMTS model, as shown in Fig. 3 , comprises three modules: the Respiratory Stages Prediction (RSP) Module, the Multimodal Teacher Knowledge Integration (MTKI) Module, and the SKL Module. The RSP module demodulates RF data into a quasi-periodic respiration curve and establishes a relationship between the ultrasound signals and respiration phases through phase analysis, thereby capturing tissue displacement caused by physiological motion in deep organs. The MTKI module reconstructs the ultrasound signals into temperature representations by incorporating HIFU acoustic parameters with the Intensity Normalization (IN) and the Stage Feature Fusion (SFF) strategy. To achieve real-time temperature prediction, the SKL module is utilized to fit the probability distribution matrix in the latent space of the MTKI module, thereby reducing the matrix computation load within the neural network and improving inference speed.

In the temperature monitoring process for deep organs, physiological movements in animals (such as breathing and heartbeat) should be considered, because they easily cause tissue deformation and displacement. Incorporating the physiological movements of animals as priors into neural networks can significantly enhance the accuracy and reliability of temperature field monitoring. Inspired by [156], we propose the RSP module. Specifically, the RSP module involves three main steps: First, the Normalized Cross Correlation (NCC) coefficient [16] was calculated on RF data to extract the respiratory phase of the animal. Second, an NCC coefficient threshold was set based on the principle of amplitude gating to preliminarily filter the RF data. Finally, small patches centered around thermocouples were established, and a gradient update strategy was used to map the relationship between the patches and respiratory stages [3].

Total
M
frames of RF data were collected during the HIFU treatment process, the first frame of the inhalation stage is selected as the reference data
X
. The NCC between
X
and all
M
frames can be shown as Eq. (1).
(1)
γ
m
=
C O V

X m

m

X m

σ
X , m
are the standard errors of
X
and
X m
.

The NCC curve
γ ( m )
of the reference data
X
is illustrated in Fig. 4 (a), where the horizontal axis represents time and the vertical axis represents the NCC coefficient. Due to the coagulative necrosis induced by HIFU treatment, the NCC coefficient gradually decreases throughout treatment. In addition, a Fourier transform was performed on the
γ ( m )
to obtain its frequency domain distribution, as shown in Fig. 4(b).. Assume that the frequencies corresponding to breathing and heartbeat are
f

Then, a low-pass filter (LPF) with cutoff frequency
f c
(
f c
= 1.3 ×

) and order
N c
is used to smooth the
γ ( m )
. As a result, the peaks
P 2
,
P 3
and other high-frequency components in
γ ′
( m )
were removed. The NCC curve
y ″
( m )
​ after filtering is shown in Fig. 4(c). In the smoothed
y ″
( m )
curve, all maxima and minima can be easily located. All maxima were designated as the boundaries of the inhalation stages and all minima as the boundaries of the exhalation stages, as shown in Fig. 4(d). Thus, each interval between two adjacent minima (or maxima) represents an exhalation (or inhalation) cycle.

The strategy above successfully establishes the correspondence between RF data and respiratory motion. Based on different physiological and mechanical respiratory processes, the respiratory cycle can generally be divided into six stages: initial inhalation, deep inhalation, end inhalation, initial exhalation, deep exhalation, and end exhalation [17]. Therefore, we calculate the complex phase
φ
m
of
y ″
( m )
and divide it into six sub-ranges, with each sub-range corresponding to a specific stage of the respiratory cycle. In this way, all RF data were uniformly divided into six groups.

Next, the coordinates of the thermocouples were used as the center point, and 16 × 16 two-dimensional patches
x ∈
R
S × H × W
were extracted from the RF data, where S, H, and W represent the number of samples, 2D patch height, and 2D patch width, respectively. Please note that three thermocouples were used in our experiment, therefore
S = 3 N
. The corresponding respiratory stages are labeled as
y r
∈
R
S × 6
, where 6 represents six stages of respiration. For respiratory prediction task, the (Multi-Layer Perceptron) [18]
ML
P r
is used to map
x ∈
R
S × H × W
as
x r
∈
R
S × 6
, where
ML
P r
is a four-layer network, with the input layer having
H × W
neurons, the hidden layer having
c × H × W
neurons, and the output layer having 6 neurons. Then Multiclass Cross-Entropy (MCE) is utilized [19] to update
ML
P r
weights, as shown in Eq. (2).
(2)
l r
= -
1 S
∑
r = 1
S
∑
i = 1
C

(

)
where
S
is the total number of samples,
C = 6
is the respiratory stage category,
y i r
denotes the actual label of the
i
sample, and
x i r
represents the probability distribution predicted by the model for the
i
sample belonging to each respiratory stage category.

Different HIFU acoustic parameters and treatment durations exert distinct effects on focal area temperature. Specifically, Increased power and pulse duration accelerate temperature rise. Shorter treatment durations influence the total energy input, thereby decreasing the rate and extent of temperature increase [2021]. Therefore, HIFU acoustic parameters and treatment durations were considered as priors in neural networks.

H =
{
H f
,
H p
,
H p d
,
H t
}
∈
R
S × 4
was denoted as the HIFU acoustic parameters and treatment durations, where
H f
is HIFU frequency,
H p
means HIFU power,
H p d
is HIFU pulse duration, and
H t
is HIFU treatment durations. Due to the different scales of HIFU acoustic parameters, directly using
H
for training without addressing these differences will limit the neural network's performance. To address this problem, an Intensity Normalization (IN) strategy is introduced into the MTKI. Formally, the
x h
∈
R
S × m
was defined as the feature maps of
H
output by the
ML
P h
, where
ML
P h
is a two-layer network, with the input layer having 4 neurons, and the output layer having
m
neurons. The IN normalizes
x h
and then use affine transformation with trainable parameters, i.e., re-scale parameters
α h
and bias parameters
β h
, as shown in Eq. (3).

Given image-annotation pairs

, where

∈
R S
is the temperature measured by thermocouple.
x ̂
h
and
x r
are high-dimensional features processed by the
MLP
, and
x
represents ultrasound features in the Cartesian coordinate system. Therefore, domain bias exists between
x ̂
h
,
x r
, and
x
. To solve this problem, a Stage Feature Fusion (SFF) strategy was proposed to integrate
x ̂
h
,
x r
, and
x
. Specifically, the SFFS process involves the following steps: First, an encoder
E h
is designed to adaptively extract ultrasound feature vectors
x
to generate
E h
( x )

R
S × ( m + 6 )
, where
( m + 6 )

S × m
and
x r

were stacked to generate

r h

S × ( m + 6 )

x ̂
r h
and perform matrix multiplication with
E h
( x )
to generate multimodal HIFU feature map
x
hifu

S × S
, as shown in Eq. (4). Finally,

S × S

The mean square error (MSE) [22] loss is computed between the prediction

, as shown in Eq. (5).

)

Real-time temperature field reconstruction enables physicians to adjust HIFU parameters based on real-time feedback during treatment. This capability helps optimize the treatment plan and improve efficiency [23]. Although the introduction of MTKI allows BMTS to learn from multimodal data (HIFU acoustic parameters, treatment duration, respiratory stage category, and ultrasound signal), the complex matrix transformations and large number of neurons still limit the inference speed of BMTS, thereby affecting the effectiveness of HIFU treatment.

To deal with above problem, an SKL module was designed to make full use of multimodal data (acoustic parameters, treatment duration, respiratory stage category, and ultrasound signal) to explore HIFU focal area temperature prediction based on ultrasound signal. Specifically, a lightweight ResNet18 [24]
E s
was defined, where
x
and
H
is the input of
E s
, and
z s
∈
R
S × S
is the feature map extracted by
E s
. Since the feature maps in the latent space represent the probability distributions output by the neural network, KL divergence can be used to establish the probabilistic relationship between
z s
and
x
hifu
, as shown in Eq. (6). By minimizing the KL divergence loss function, the relative entropy between
z s
and
x
hifu
can be reduced, thereby causing the distribution of
z s
in the latent space to gradually approach that of
x
hifu
.
(6)
l k
=
1
S 2
∑ i S
∑ j S
z
ij
l o g
(
x
hifu
ij
z
ij
)

Finally, a projection mapping strategy is used to map
z s
to
y s
∈
R S
, then utilize MSE loss function for temperature fitting, as shown in Eq. (7).
(7)
l s
=
1 S
∑
i = 1
S
(
y i s
-
y i t
)

SKL contains the feature and the potential relationship learned from multimodal data (acoustic parameters, treatment duration, respiratory stage category, and ultrasound signal) by RSP and MTKI. Therefore, SKL can directly reconstruct the two-dimensional temperature field within the HIFU focal area of deep organs (overcomes the effects of physiological movements).

During the training phase, the coordinates of thermocouples were used as central points to extract 16 × 16 2D patches from the ultrasound data, applying the corresponding temperature as labels. At the inference phase, a sliding window strategy was employed on the ultrasound data to extract 16 × 16 2D patches with a 90 % overlap, and the SKL module was employed to reconstruct the HIFU focal area temperature field. The neural network was implemented by PyTorch, and the experiments were conducted on a workstation equipped with an NVIDIA RTX3090Ti with 24 GB of RAM. All models were trained using the Adam optimizer, with a maximum of 10,000 iterations, an initial learning rate of 1e-3, and an exponential decay rate of 0.99. The batch size and the number of epochs were set to 32 and 100, respectively.

In the METHOD.3.1 section, the establishment of a mapping relationship between RF data and respiration phases using RSP combined with phase analysis is discussed. Specifically, a one-hot encoding strategy is employed for data labeling and the RSP prediction results are categorized into true positive (TP), true negative (TN), false positive (FP), and false negative (FN) through a multiclassification task, then precision, recall, accuracy, and F1 score can be calculated as shown in Table 1 . Fig. 5 visually illustrates six different respiration stages, where Fig. 5(a)-(c) correspond to three inhalation stages and Fig. 5(d)-(f) correspond to three exhalation stages. By integrating the data from Table 1 and Fig. 5, even though exhalation (or inhalation) images are in highly similar states, the RSP can still accurately differentiate each stage. During the experiment, we found that adaptively selecting the area of interest using the cross-correlation method would increase the neural network's inference time without significantly improving prediction accuracy. Therefore, no subsequent motion compensation was performed based on the RSP prediction results.

Because of the influence of breathing and heartbeat, echoes from deep organs usually contain significant noise, leading to relatively low precision in temperature monitoring. In order to enhance the temperature measurement accuracy, it is of great importance to eliminate the physiological noise. Fig. 6 illustrates the ability of the BMTS model and two variants to overcome physiological noise in reconstructing the two-dimensional temperature field of a New Zealand rabbit liver. The two variants were considered:
BMT
S
RSP
, which removes the MTKI module from the BMTS; and
BMT
S
MTKI
, which removes the RSP module from the BMTS.

Fig. 6(a)-(c) corresponds to the heating stage. As the HIFU treatment progresses, the spatial extent of the temperature field expands, and the color scale of the thermal map intensifies. Fig. 6(d)-(f) shows the cooling stage, during which the HIFU treatment ceases, causing the focal area temperature to gradually decrease. Therefore, the radiation range of the temperature field contracts, and the color scale of the thermal map diminishes. Due to factors such as physiological motion, acoustic diffraction, and tissue heterogeneity, artifacts effects are observed in the monition results on temperature distribution of
BMT
S
RSP
and
BMT
S
MTKI
, as indicated by the green arrow in Fig. 6(c). Among them,
BMT
S
RSP
exhibits superior artifact reduction compared with
BMT
S
MTKI
, primarily because it effectively establishes a functional relationship between ultrasound echoes and physiological motion through phase analysis techniques, thereby enhancing its resistance to physiological noise. Surprisingly,
BMT
S
MTKI
also demonstrates a certain degree of resistance to physiological noise. This may be because the MTKI module provides guidance to the SKL module, helping it rapidly identify a suitable parameter space during the initial training stage, thereby reducing the impact of physiological noise. In comparison to
BMT
S
RSP
and
BMT
S
MTKI
, BMTS exhibits the most outstanding performance in resisting physiological noise, as shown by no significant artifacts in the area indicated by the green arrow. This qualitatively demonstrates the effectiveness of BMTS in resisting physiological noise.

In addition, the interpolated intensity profile of the green dashed line (Fig. 6(c)) was presented in order to confirm the relationship between the monitored temperature field dimensions and the predetermined focal area dimensions, as shown in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7(a)-(f) represent the lateral and axial interpolated intensity profile of the reconstructed temperature distribution within HIFU focal area results for BMTS,
BMT
S
RSP
, and
BMT
S
MTKI
, respectively. The temperature field area reconstructed by BMTS shows no significant sidelobes or artifacts, the axial and lateral lengths of which are approximately 8 mm and 2.2 mm, respectively, aligning with the predetermined dimensions (axial length ∼8 mm and lateral length ∼2 mm).
BMT
S
RSP
exhibits a spurious peak (Fig. 7(c), indicated by the green arrow), primarily due to physiological noise from deep organs. The axial and lateral lengths of its focal area are approximately 10.5 mm and 2.6 mm, respectively, showing a considerable deviation from the predetermined focal area dimensions. Meanwhile, the spurious peak in
BMT
S
MTKI
(Fig. 7(e), indicated by the green arrow) is more pronounced. Additionally, the axial length (11.8 mm) and lateral length (3.3 mm) of the focal area show significant deviation from the predetermined dimensions.

We compared the actual temperature (dashed lines) with the temperatures predicted by BMTS (solid lines), as illustrated in Fig. 8 (a). It is easy to find that BMTS exhibits excellent performance during HIFU heating and tissue cooling process with an average error of 0.8 °C. Notably, the highest temperature measured by our proposed method can reach at 67 °C, which is the temperature record for ultrasound thermometry. At 67 °C, the maximum error occurs, the value of which is only 1.5 °C, acceptable for clinical use.

This success is primarily due to the ability of RSP and MTKI modules, which enable adaptively capturing ultrasound echo signals and efficiently processing them through feature weighting, vector space sharing, and dynamic weight adjustment techniques. Therefore, different from traditional backscattering (univariate analysis) methods, BMTS integrates multiple variables—such as blood flow, tissue displacement, tissue characteristics, HIFU acoustic parameters, and treatment duration—to establish a mapping relationship between ultrasound echo signals and temperature, thereby enabling effective prediction in broader-temperature range.

To verify the roles of the RSP and MTKI modules in high-temperature (>50 °C) monition, we systematically conduct a comparative analysis. Compared with BMTS, the performances of
BMT
S
RSP
(Fig. 8(b)) and
BMT
S
MTKI
(Fig. 8(c)) are obviously inferior. At the peak temperature (67 °C), the measurement errors are 3.7 °C and 3.5 °C, respectively. Meanwhile, in the whole temperature range, the average error of the former is 2.1 and that of the latter is 1.8. The MTKI module enables establishing an integrated relationship between acoustic parameters and ultrasound echo signals, and guiding the SKL module by KL divergence strategy. Thus, without MTKI module, it is difficult to capture these prior features, thereby impairing its ability to handle temperature monition, especially for high temperature (>50 °C) tasks. Additionally, when the temperature induced by HIFU exceeds 50 °C, the tissue will undergo hardness increasing due to protein denaturation [25], leading to relatively strong temporal and spatial coherence in signal noise. In contrast, the noise resulting from physiological motion exhibits relatively weak temporal and spatial coherence. Without RSP module, it is hard to extract feature parameters related to both tissue denaturation and physiological motion, thus limiting its capability to monitor temperature.

The time consumption of the SKL module at various scales for reconstructing the two-dimensional temperature field of an animal liver was analyzed to demonstrate the real-time capability of the BMTS model. Note that the reconstructed temperature field is only 20 pixels × 35 pixels, and the results in the RESULTS section were subjected to super-resolution processing [26], which is not included in this time calculation. As shown in Table 2 , up to 20 SKL modules can be processed in parallel on a 24 GB 3090Ti workstation, achieving a reconstruction speed of 0.373 s and meeting clinical standards.

However, constrained by hardware limitations, we only employed an offline real-time temperature monitoring strategy. Specifically, dynamic online real-time temperature monitoring (DORTM) requires a high-performance data acquisition system capable of processing real-time data from multiple sensors [2728]. Additionally, DORTM necessitates the transmission of large volumes of data. Insufficient network bandwidth can delay data transmission, compromising real-time performance. A potential solution is to develop efficient transmission and computation systems to reduce data transfer latency and prevent overflow.

As described in Section METHOD 3.2, the acoustic parameters of HIFU influence thermal deposition in the focal area [29,30]. Therefore, the acoustic power, pulse repetition period, duty cycle, and number of pulses during the HIFU treatment process were adjusted. Specifically, the acoustic parameters were categorized into several scenarios: Scenario 1: Acoustic power of 95 W, pulse repetition period of 800 ms, duty cycle of 50 %, and 300 pulses. Scenario 2: Acoustic power of 95 W, pulse repetition period of 800 ms, duty cycle of 50 %, and 350 pulses. Scenario 3: Acoustic power of 85 W, pulse repetition period of 800 ms, duty cycle of 50 %, and 250 pulses. Scenario 4: Acoustic power of 90 W, pulse repetition period of 800 ms, duty cycle of 50 %, and 400 pulses. As observed in Table 3 , the maximum error between the predicted and actual temperature in the four scenarios is less than 2 °C, and the average error is less than 1 °C. This also validates our hypothesis that incorporating HIFU acoustic parameters and treatment duration as priors in the neural network can enhance its generalization capability. The p-value calculations were derived from Scenarios 1–4 in comparison to Scenario 0 (acoustic power of 90 W, pulse repetition period of 800 ms, duty cycle of 50 %, and 300 pulses). The Mann-Whitney U test was used to ensure robust statistical evaluation, with significance levels indicated by “**” for p < 0.01 and “*” for p < 0.001.

In summary, by learning the mapping between the ultrasound echo signal and the temperature with a respiratory stages prediction module and the multimodal teacher knowledge integration module, two-dimension temperature distribution within HIFU focal area in rabbet’s liver has been accurately reconstructed with an average error and a frame speed of 0.8 °C and 0.37 s, respectively. Most importantly, we have successfully expanded the maximum measurable temperature of ultrasonic thermometry to a record value of 67 °C, filling the gap in noninvasive high temperature (>50 °C) measurement. It is believable that the development of AI-powered ultrasonics thermometry is beneficial for precise HIFU therapy planning in the future.

Shunyao Luan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yongshuo Ji: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yumei Liu: Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Data curation. Linling Zhu: Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Hong Zhao: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Haoyu Zhou: Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis. Ke Li: Visualization, Validation, Data curation. Weizhen Zhu: Visualization, Validation, Data curation. Benpeng Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.
